responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Jane Milton

Jane Milton

Jane Milton  

This issue of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy divides neatly into three sections. The papers by Hughes and Gibson address the challenge of making psychotherapy teaching alive and interesting for medical students. They first courageously inquired how their students perceived the current teaching of this subject. Undaunted, and stung into action, by the comment ‘How can you make such an interesting subject so dull?’, they set about, with the help of a professional educationist, planning and delivering a new syllabus, with good effects on the morale and satisfaction of all concerned.

The two clinical papers which follow both address areas within the public sector where psychoanalytic psychotherapy has evident potential but which to date has impinged little. Freeman and Kells give an unusual and vivid account of the psychotherapy of a patient referred to a dental hospital with dysmorphophobic preoccupations about her teeth, coupled with severe anxieties about dental treatment. We are shown how psychotherapy helped to address both issues. Cregeen describes work with some young people with severe epilepsy in a residential setting, discussing the complex interplay of physical and psychological factors in these patients, and their predicament as people living with recurrent and unpredictable experiences of collapse and fragmentation.

The last two papers address the much broader issues of the total setting in which our work takes place. Bell's concern is with a current political climate and culture which he sees as anti-dependency to an extreme degree, and which has spawned the Market-led public services. He explains, giving some clinical parallells, how he sees this as omnipotently denying ordinary human need, and running worryingly counter to the values which we espouse as psychoanalytic psychotherapists and as Health Service clinicians generally. Finally, Mishan addresses our attitude to the planet itself. Drawing particularly on the work of Money-Kyrle, and, like Bell, thinking about primitive defensive and destructive processes, he also discusses our tendency dangerously to deny basic need and dependency, and to attack and spoil the basic structures we need for our continuing existence.

[[RunningHead]]

Paranoia: New Psychoanalytical Perspectives. Edited by John M OldhamStanley Bone London:International Universities Press Pp. 174 $27.50.

John Lawrence

John Lawrence

John Lawrence  

This book is a collection of papers, by Americans except for one by Hanna Segal. Although all are written from a psychoanalytic point of view, there are at least three different schools of thought represented: Kleinian, Ego Psychology and Self Psychology. This enables us to compare and contrast the three different theoretical viewpoints as they apply in the different clinical contexts presented. However, some readers may find the papers do not always contain sufficient background theory, especially for those, like me, who have only slender familiarity with Ego Psychology and Self Psychology. Nevertheless, the papers cover a wide range of clinical topics, and where the writers present clinical material, it is usually clear how the writer is thinking about his patients.

Devised in three sections, the book opens with four papers grouped under the head ‘Theoretical and Developmental Aspects of Paranoia’. Two of these are clinically based. Section Two, ‘Paranoia in Organisational and Social Systems’, includes a Kernberg paper on leadership styles, but overall I found this section the least interesting. Section Three, ‘Paranoia in Clinical Work’ also has four papers, and one of the authors, Arnold Goldberg, writes from a Self Psychology standpoint. I shall not attempt a summary of each paper, but make some detailed and some general points in the hope of providing the potential reader with a flavour of this collection.

79

Nicola Blandford

Nicola Blandford

Nicola Blandford  

It is a pleasure to read a book like this from a general practitioner. There is also a sadness, reflected in the book, that this sort of approach in primary care may become harder and harder to sustain in the face of the demands put on family doctors by NHS ‘reforms’, fundholding, new contracts and increasing shortages of new recruits to the specialty.

John Holland has been a GP in Wansford, near Peterborough, for many years. He has been oriented towards a psychodynamic approach, both in his dealings with patients and in his work as a trainer of new GPs.

responseSet.title: A Dysmorphophobic Reaction to Cosmetic Dentistry: Observations and Responses to Psychotherapeutic Intervention
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Dysmorphophobic Reaction to Cosmetic Dentistry: Observations and Responses to Psychotherapeutic Intervention

Ruth Freeman and Briony Kells

Briony Kells

Belfast BT12 6BP.

Briony Kells  

This paper gives an account of the psychoanalytic psychotherapy of a 23-year-old woman who sustained a dysmorphophobic reaction during and following cosmetic dental treatment. The data which emerged during the psychotherapeutic treatment is described in detail, attention being concentrated on memories, phantasies and transference reactions. A sexual theory of symptom-formation is described which provides an explanation for the patient's anomalous reaction to the dental treatment. The successful outcome of the psychotherapy is attributed to a number of factors, each of which had its own special importance.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Imagining Characters: Six Conversations about Women Writers. By A.S. ByattIgnê Sodré London: Chatto & Windus pp.268 £16.99

Margaret Rustin

Margaret Rustin

Margaret Rustin  

This book offers the fruits of a learned and immensely lively collaboration between the novelist and literary critic, A.S. Byatt, and the psychoanalyst Ignês Sodré. Byatt's literary exploration of unconscious processes will be known to readers of her novel

276

Possession, a story which unravels the impact of unconsious projective identification. Sodré's clinical insight and passion for literature make for a splendid pair of minds at work. The two met to talk over six classic novels by women writers, and their dialogue centres on the characters and relationships explored in their chosen texts.

The novels they selected were intended to span the whole period of great novel-writing, and to provide aní pportunity for reflection on cultural and hsitorical themes, as well as the exploration of character. They offer a reading of Jane Austen's Mansfield Park, Charlotte Bronte's Villette, George Elliot's Daniel Deronda, Willa Cather's The Professor's House, Iris Murdoch's An Unofficial Rose and Toni Morrison's Beloved. A final chapter discusses the relationship between ‘Dreams and Fictions’, and describes their understanding of each other's approach to literature. They share a fasciantion with the human mind's need for fictions, and a belief in the importance of the distinction between defensive day-dreaming and the psychic work of both dreamers and writers. Dreams and works of art are seeen as both representing aspects of the internal world in symbolic form. Their dialogue addresses Byatt's experience of herself as a reader and writer of fiction and some of the similarities between the analytic couple and the writer/reader dyad. Their immense enjoyment of the books they talked about, and of each other's ideas, offers a delightful backdrop to the challenge of what they have to say.

The book is not an easy one to review, since appreciating the intricate and absorbing details of their conversations depends on a good knowledge of the works concerned. As an enthusiastic reader of novels, I started out with a prior acquaintance with five of the six, but I felt that to get the most out of this book, I should have re-read the lot, and added the missing one, since their chapter persuaded me that I had unfairly neglected Iris Murdoch's writing. There is a plot summary at the opening of each chapter, to help the reader, but this really serves only as a useful reminder of the basic story-line, so this adds up to a caveat relevant to the readership of this journal - to reap fully the rewards the book has to offer, a lot of reading-time is needed! A splendid project for a long summer holiday, or an on-going reading group.

responseSet.title: Meeting the Challenge: Teaching Psychotherapy to Medical Students: Part I: Students and Syllabus
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Meeting the Challenge: Teaching Psychotherapy to Medical Students

Part I: Students and Syllabus

Patricia Hughes and Christopher Gibson

Christopher Gibson

St George's Hospital Medical School
Cranmer Terrace London SW17 ORE.

51 Winchester Avenue London NW6 7TT.

Christopher Gibson  

Staff in the department were dissatisfied with the standard of their teaching and determined that psychotherapy should become an interesting subject for their students. Evaluation of the teaching showed that there was no stated syllabus to guide either teachers or students, and definition of the syllabus was the first task in reorganising the teaching. Effective reorganisation needed consideration of the nature and needs of the medical students, and some thought about what is relevant for them as future doctors. Teachers had to think from the students’ position and define a syllabus which is understandable to the students, and not assume that what is clear to the teacher is necessarily so to the students.

The undergraduate medical curriculum is currently changing, and all subjects are subject to rigorous scrutiny to determine whether or not they will continue to have a place in the new curriculum. If psychotherapists believe that their subject should be taught to medical students they must present a coherent syllabus which is demonstrably relevant to medical undergraduates.

responseSet.title: The Interaction of the Schizophrenic and the Milieu in the Acute Psychiatric Ward
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Interaction of the Schizophrenic and the Milieu in the Acute Psychiatric Ward

Martin Lotz

Martin Lotz

Skovrødvej 11, DK-3460 Birkerød, Denmark.

Martin Lotz  

The interaction of the schizophrenic with the milieu of the acute ward is discussed from a psychoanalytic point of view. In the acute phases of the disease, the schizophrenic experiences internal chaos. While he is undergoing such internal stress, he will constantly be trying to adapt globally to the milieu, and he will seek relief by transposing (protectively) his chaotic feelings onto everyone on the ward. This will affect and stress members of staff. As they are affected by this stress, interactional patterns-involving both staff and patients may emerge: chaos, splitting and persecutory patterns. Chaos is seen as the primary reaction, while splitting and persecutory patterns seem to arise from within the staff. If the members of staff are not attuned to the pathology of the patient, they may react in such a way as to aggravate the problem. It is unfortunate if the unconscious feelings of guilt in individual staff-members, for appearing to persecute the patient, lead to their ‘acting out’. During the initial stages of hospitalisation, the schizophrenic seems to need the help of a psychotherapeutic approach from the multi-disciplinary team to alleviate his inner chaos. However, during the later stages, he will be better helped by more limited contact with a responsible key-worker, or, even better, a psychotherapist in a regular psychotherapy programme adapted to his needs.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Unconcious Logic. An introduction to Matte Blanco's Bi-Logic and its Uses. By Eric RaynerRoutledge. Pp. 180 £40.00 hb; £14.99 pb.

Margaret Arden

Margaret Arden

Margaret Arden  

This book will interest people who have come across bi-logic, but have felt daunted by Matte-Blanco's own writing. Eric Rayner was the first to generate support for Matte-Blanco, with his 1981 paper in the International Journal of Psycho-Analysis. Since then, he has been the leading-light of the London Bi-Logic Group, which, although small, has attracted members with a wide variety of interests who have seen the creative possibilities in Matte-Blanco's work. Without this focus, Matte-Blanco would have remained unknown outside his native Chile and Rome, where he spent the last thirty years of his life.

Ignacio Matte-Blanco (1908-1995) spent many years as a psychiatrist in Chile, studying the speech of schizophrenic patients. He trained as a psychoanalyst in London in the late 1930s, before returning to Chile. While in England, he also studied mathematical logic. He wrote two books, The Unconscious as Infinite Sets (1975) and Thinking, Feeling and Being (1988), in which he expounded his theory of the mind. Starting with Freud's two principles of mental functioning, primary and secondary process, he elaborated in detail how these represent two different logics. He described primary process as symmetrical thinking, in contrast to the differentiating asymmetry of rational secondary-process thought. The term bi-logic denotes the combination of these two processes in all mental activity. For example, although dreams are the purest example of symmetrical thinking we have, they often contain elements of rational thought.

Matte-Blanco's bi-logic has stimulated some people to think in a new way about primary and secondary process. For them, it has been an illumination of psychoanalytic theory and a creative approach to understanding the unconscious mind. People who respond positively to Matte-Blanco are likely to write papers which develop their own applications of his theory. For the majority, however, Matte-Blanco remains something of a puzzle: his obsessionality and single-mindedness, together with the difficulty of understanding his thinking, often make people give up. This book is invaluable as an introduction, an explanation of bi-logic, and a guide to Matte-Blanco's influence on others.

responseSet.title: Psychotic Metaphysics. By Eric Rhode London: Clunie, Karnac. pp.325 £14.95.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychotic Metaphysics. By Eric Rhode London: Clunie, Karnac. pp.325 £14.95.

Jessica Sacret

Jessica Sacret

Jessica Sacret  

This book takes the form of a sustained reverie, linking in an associative rather than didactic way ideas stemming from the more philosophical reaches of post-Kleinian thought. The central theme is that of the psychic journey from foetal to depressive position functioning and the catastrophic nature of change. Ideas and illustrations drawn from social anthropology, myth, primitive and Western art and sculpture, literature, aesthetics and philosophy, as well as from the author's own personal and clinical experience, interweave in a rich tapestry — sometimes perhaps too rich — in the task of substantiating a thesis on the nature of thought, psychic function and a theory of psychosis. The occasional references to a paper of Klein, or a quotation from Bion, stand out like lonely signposts towards thinking that one thankfully recognises as familiar.

The underlying conceptual framework expresses the rationalist view of mind underpinning an exploration of foetal thought; and the structuralist binary division theory of meaning of the anthropologist Lévi-Strauss, whereby meaning is said to arise from differential relationships between opposed elements within a system. These theoretical notions, alluded to but never spelt out, inform the central themes amidst a wealth of idea and imagery, so that the effect is considerably fragmented, if not psychotic. It is as if a Pandora's-box of ideas has burst open, creating, in this reader at least, a combination of fascination and frustration: fascination because of the richness and extensiveness of the ideas and the obvious erudition of the author; frustration because of the way one is left struggling to make connections and links that are often only tantalisingly implied. This effect, however, is presumably intended, since a central concern of the book is the Bionic notion of the turbulence that is at the heart of catastrophic change.

One important function of the book is to draw the reader's attention to some of the metaphysical questions underlying the psychoanalytic enquiry. These are areas where notably Bion (1965), Money-Kyrle (1978) and Meltzer (1988) have wandered. Prolonged clinical work with patients, particularly with those who are very disturbed inevitably leads to speculations concerning the nature of mind. Here psychoanalysis enters into dialogue with epistemology.

responseSet.title: Paranoia or Adaptation? The Implications of a Kings Fund Review on a Psychotherapy Department
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Paranoia or Adaptation? The Implications of a Kings Fund Review on a Psychotherapy Department

Antony Garelick and Jan Wiener

Jan Wiener

Woodford Bridge, Woodford Green, Essex QG8 8BY

Woodford Bridge, Woodford Green, Essex QG8 8BY

Jan Wiener  

This paper examines the impact of a Kings Fund Review on an established psychotherapy clinic during a period of major change in NHS mental-health services resulting from government initiatives. Some of the political, social and personal pressures on psychotherapists and their effects on the structure and process of the Clinic are considered. The benefits of the Review in helping Clinic staff to identify and distinguish between those initiatives to ‘fight’ and those where ‘adaptation’ is appropriate are illustrated. Through an understanding of the unconscious processes involved, strategy and tactics employed to maintain the identity, core values and therapeutic integrity of the Clinic are discussed.

[[RunningHead]]

Meeting the Challenge: Teaching Psychotherapy to Medical Students

Part II: Style and Delivery

Patricia Hughes and Christopher Gibson

Christopher Gibson

St George's Hospital Medical School
Cranmer Terrace London SW17 ORE.

51 Winchester Avenue London NW6 7TT.

Christopher Gibson  

In order to improve the quality of their teaching to medical students, the staff of an NHS psychotherapy department engaged a professional teacher to demonstrate how teaching could be delivered more effectively and in a way which would help the students to learn and would engage their interest. This paper outlines the structure which the educationist suggested for the teaching, with particular emphasis on the need for the teacher to plan ahead and to organise both the content and methods of individual sessions and the course as a whole. Creating a coherent structure gives the students firm boundaries within which to think and reflect on what they are learning. The psychotherapists have found this input from an educationist invaluable, and the student teaching has improved markedly.

Training Analysis in the Dutch Society for Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy: A Survey of Attitudes and Feasibility11

Rutger Trijsburg

Rutger Trijsburg

Universiteit. Molewaterplein 50, 3000 DR Rotterdam, Netherlands.

Rutger Trijsburg  

As is the case in many training courses in psychoanalytic psychotherapy, one of the training requirements of the Dutch Society for Psychoanalytical Psychotherapy (NVPP) is a training analysis, currently a minimum duration of 700 hours. During the last few years, this requirement has become somewhat controversial. Because the NVPP does not have information about the current interest in NVPP membership, the Board of the NVPP decided to do a survey. Of 995 psychiatrists, clinical psychologists and psychotherapists, who had recently completed their training, or were still in training, 623 filled in a questionnaire. Of those who are interested in the NVPP training, 39 per cent judged the training analysis as not feasible in terms of time, and 61 per cent in terms of money. Forms of personal treatment thought desirable for anyone who wishes to become a psychoanalytic psychotherapist at a specialist level are, in descending order, psychoanalytic psychotherapy (63%), psychoanalysis (39%), psychoanalytic group psychotherapy (25%), and psychoanalytic marital or family therapy (6%). Respondents who judge personal analysis as not feasible, also tend to judge psychoanalysis to be equivalent to other forms of psychoanalytic psychotherapy, whereas those who judge personal analysis as feasible, tend to think that personal analysis is essential for a psychoanalytic psychotherapist at the specialist level.

How to Survive as a Psychotherapist. By Nina ColtartSheldon Press. Pp.120

Robert Tollemache

Robert Tollemache

Robert Tollemache  

I have enjoyed this unusual and highly readable book. Part of its pleasure is Dr Coltart's distinctive writing ‘voice’ — strong, quirky, courageous and individual — which binds he book together. For example, patients for assessment who ring up and say, ‘Is that Nina?’ find that she becomes cool, even frosty; and she reports saying to one very difficult patient for assessment, ‘I don't recall saying you might call me Nina’. I therefore hasten to call her ‘Dr Coltart’.

She tells us that as she went on she realised that she would have to be more autobiographical than she anticipated. There is room for much more writing by psychotherapists about their own personal approach to life and work as a psychotherapist, among some of the more po-faced and boring of the rest of the literature.

[[RunningHead]]

Special Care Babies and their Developing Relationships. By Anne McFadyen London: Pp. 194. Routledge.

Judith Jackson

Judith Jackson

Judith Jackson  

There has been very little literature on premature babies of very low birth-weight from a psychological perspective. This book is an attempt to fill that gap, and is a development of some detailed and very interesting research work carried out by Anne McFadyen whilst training as a Child & Adolescent psychiatrist at the Tavistock Clinic. In particular, McFadyen sets out to explore the earliest relationships as they emerge within the special-care baby unit, using the psychoanalytic infant-observation method as her main research tool, together with an examination of mothers’ narratives.

The book is divided into two sections. Part I provides a theoretical overview. The writer discusses parent-child relationships from different perspectives, bringing together psychoanalysis, ethology and child-development theory and research. She then tries to make links between these theoretical ideas and aspects of special-care babies which deserve closer attention. While many books have been devoted to the psychological aspects of neonatal intensive care, McFadyen makes the point that most reports end up talking about the adults involved, instead of trying to take account of the neonate's experience and unique contribution to the developing relationship from the very beginning of life. She reminds us (and later on in the book shows us) how difficult it is to think about and empathise with a special-care baby — even more so for the professionals than for the parents. In a busy special-care baby unit, the professionals involved do not have the time to spend even a few minutes really tuning in to what any particular baby is feeling:

How can any of us begin to put ourselves in the place of these babies, some of whom are fragile, scraggy-looking creatures who may be naked and blindfolded, who are connected to tubes and wires to keep them alive, and who cannot be cuddled or loved in the same way as ordinary babies?

(p. 37).

The Motherhood Constellation. By Daniel Stern New York: Basic Books. pp. 229 £19.95 (hardback).

Paul Barrows

Paul Barrows

Paul Barrows  

Daniel Stern has subtitled this, his latest book, ‘A Unified View of Parent-Infant Psychotherapy’. His aim is to bring together the different strands that constitute the growing field of Infant Psychiatry, and to suggest an over-arching hypothesis that she should inform them all.

In Part One Stern gives a survey of the constituent elements of the ‘Clinical System’ that is created when therapist meets patient. These are the parents’ and the infant's mental representations, the actions of these participants and the representations and actions that the therapist introduces into the situation. He reviews a wide range of the literature that provides models for understanding these different elements.

In Part Two Stern presents a review (acknowledged to be incomplete) of different forms of parent-infant psychotherapy categorised according to their ‘Port of Entry’ into this clinical system; and then looks at the commonalities among the different approaches.

In Part Three he suggests that a unique psychic constellation is in operation at this time of early infancy: the triad of grandmother-mother-baby displacing the oedipal triad as the preeminent organising principle of mental life.

175

[[RunningHead]]

The Tavistock and Group-Analytic Approaches to Group Psychotherapy: A Trainee's Perspective11

Arturo Ezquerro

Arturo Ezquerro

57 Hamlet Square, London NW2 1SR.

Arturo Ezquerro  

This paper examines some issues related to the Tavistock (Bionian) and Group-Analytic (Foulkesian) approaches to group therapy, in the context of an out-patient group which met weekly over thirty-two months. Reference is made to theories and models within the frameworks of Psychoanalysis and Group-Analysis.

To conduct this group was a part of the author's training at the Institute of Group Analysis; although the group met at the Tavistock Clinic (‘next door’). The author had previously trained for four years in Child & Adolescent Psychiatry at the Tavistock; and he negotiated this unusual set-up with the Tavistock Clinic's management, becoming the first Institute trainee to run such a group at the Tavistock.

The author had two supervisors simultaneously; hence there was a constant tension between the two theories throughout the history of the group. The Tavistock's supervisor played an active part in the early stages, especially during the process of setting-up the group; his role progressively changed to that of overseeing the work. On the other hand, supervision at the Institute was ninety minutes weekly for three years, in a small group of up to four trainees.

In contrast to usual Group-Analytic practice, on the advice of the Tavistock's supervisor, eleven patients were invited to join the group without meeting the conductor beforehand. In contrast to usual Tavistock practice, on the advice of the Institute supervisor, one group member was seen, after she had left the group, for individual psychotherapy by the conductor. Being ‘caught up in the middle’, the conductor himself was stretched to breaking-point. But he survived, and every one of the eleven members with him.

Making Sense: Brain Trauma, Epileptic Seizures and Personal Meaning

Simon Cregeen

Simon Cregeen

Adcote House, Columbia Road, Birkenhead, Merseyside, L43 6TU.

Simon Cregeen  

Although epilepsy is a relatively common condition, psychoanalytic thinkers seem to have written little on either the psychological factors which are sometimes implicated in the aetiology of seizure events, or on the emotional ramifications for the person living with the experience of having seizures. This paper outlines a few issues and ideas concerned with the psychodynamics of epilepsy, and then discusses some aspects of clinical work with two patients who were working to understand themselves in the light of a seizure condition. For each of them, the exploration of fantasies arising from their experience of themselves as having damaged brains enabled them to make some sense of personal events and feelings. This paper is a grouping of ideas related to some of the emotional aspects of living with brain trauma and seizures. It is intended to encourage and contribute to thinking in this area of clinical work.

Bearing the Unbearable - Working with the Abused Mind

Roger Kennedy

Roger Kennedy

The Cassel Hospital
Richmond, Surrey, TW10 7JF.

Roger Kennedy  

The paper discusses some issues concerned with the psychoanalytic treatment of adults who have memories of being sexually abused as children. Issues covered include the nature of the abused mind, the nature of memories of abuse and the way that the abusing experience may be repeated in the analysis by making an emotional impact of a particular kind on the analyst. The title refers to how the analyst may need to help the patient bear unbearable experiences which the immature abused child could not deal with effectively. Clinical material from an adult analysis is presented to highlight the latter theme. But, in addition, the author's extensive experience of working with severely broken-down families, who are admitted as in-patients to the Family Unit of the Cassel Hospital, London, is also drawn on in order to clarify some of the difficult issues that arise from working with the abused patient.

Conformism as a Symptom of Adolescent Breakdown: A Clinical Case11

Anthony Bateman

Anthony Bateman

St Ann's Hospital, St Ann's Road, London N15 3TH.

Anthony Bateman  

Adolescent breakdown may take two forms. Firstly a pervasive negative identity and secondly an inappropriate conformism. An adult patient is described whose conformism in adolescence implied good adjustment but left her unable to develop an independent sense of herself. This left her vulnerable in later life. Failure to recognise adolescent problems, especially in conforming individuals, may itself be a component of adolescent breakdown since a primary task of such adolescents is to appear capable and independent.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Making of an Analyst: A Memoir. By Michael FordhamFree Association Books pp. 151 £14.95.

Nicola Blandford

Nicola Blandford

Nicola Blandford  

When reviewing The Fenceless Field, a collection of Michael Fordham's papers, for this Journal recently (vol.9, 1995, 301-306), Richard Carvalho complained at the way Jungians are offered Jungian books to review, Middle-Groupers middle-groups books, Kleinians Kleinian books, and so on. He felt this perpetuated the divisions between us, keeping us all ‘fenced’ inside our fields.

And I, another Jungian, reviewing Michael Fordham's memoir, tend to agree, and to wonder how many of the younger analysts and psychotherapists from other schools will have heard of Fordham. They will probably not know his paper, given at the 1959 Symposium on Countertransference held by the Medical Section of the British Psychological Society. The other speakers were Paula Heimann, Margaret Little, Ruth Strauss and D W Winnicott, who, despite coming from different schools, had much in common in their thinking about the analytic attitude.

Dr Michael Fordham died in April 1995. A figure of immense importance to British Jungians, he had been instrumental in the establishment of the Society of Analytical Psychology (SAP) in London as a training body for both Adult and Child analysts. He had co-edited (with Gerhardt Adler and Herbert Read) the English translation of Jung's Collected Works and had been the founding editor of the Journal of Analytical Psychology. He was an innovative theoretician, using Kleinian ideas to open out and challenge Jung's views of the psyche in childhood, creating an entirely original theory of the process of the development of the self. Jung's theory of individuation could be shown to apply right from the beginning of life, not just in the second half of adulthood, as Jung himself had suggested.

The Making of an Analyst is an autobiographical memoir describing both the public and private sides of a richly-led life. Fordham came from a minor branch of a family of East Anglian land-owning gentry. The sense of Fordhams being special seems to have stayed with him all his life (‘I could never understand that what I had done was other than what a Fordham would ordinarily do’ (p. 5)). There are passages in the book when this rather narcissistic stance is off-putting. Fordham several times describes the difficulty he had in accepting praise: it reads oddly. However, in his preface, he disarmingly writes of how an autobiography can be ‘a sort of lie about oneself’, and that he has tried to be truthful. In describing the course of his life — its successes, vicissitudes, idiosyncracies and failures — Fordham shows us his process of individuation, the ‘becoming’ that is implied in the title.

Peter Whewell

Peter Whewell

Peter Whewell  

Unimaginable Storms is the progeny of two psychoanalysts from very different backgrounds. Dr Murray Jackson has had many years of experience in psychiatry and psychotherapy, and his book is about the inpatient ward he ran, for over a decade, at the Maudsley Hospital in collaboration with Professor Robert Cawley. The book's originality derives from the detailed transcripts of interviews between Dr Jackson and his patients, as recorded by Paul Williams, who has a background in anthropology, and who was an observer on the ward over a two-year period. The message of the book is that psychodynamic thinking should be at the heart of psychiatric management of the disturbed, psychotic individual and that consideration of the form of the disturbance is empty if the content of the illness is not given meaning. Of particular importance is the diagnostic assessment interview to which the patient transcripts relate.

Susan Phillips

Susan Phillips

Susan Phillips  

The essays in this book honour the psychoanalyst Robert Wallerstein, whose work as a teacher and researcher in psychotherapy has been very influential in the American clinical culture. As some writing by European and South American psychoanalysts is included, the book offers a useful opportunity to compare diverse theoretical views illustrated with clinical material on the crucial topic of psychic change.

The editors suggest that the current concern is with how and why change occurs psychologically, and what is specific to psychoanalytical work. They suggest there is more interest in studying therapeutic effect than in debating the classification of psychopathology. Unless there is structural change, they say, defined as ‘systems of enduring and slowly changing meanings’, it is questionable whether resources will be made available for the work. As we are well placed to know, there is intense resistance within the individual, fuelled by the compulsion to repeat, which works against change. Presumably a similar opposition is reflected in political arguments over the provision of psychotherapy to deal with such potent yet invisible forces. If simple support or the passage of time effect change, what can justify our long, often-slow psychoanalytic work? How much could be left to these and other non-analytic factors to modify distress? What is specific to psychoanalytic work? Wallerstein's studies have tried to deal with these issues; so it follows that this book offers a variety of approaches to them, and could be helpful in allowing us articulately to join the debate.

88

responseSet.title: Letter to the Editor
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter to the Editor

Bob Hinshelwood and Sue Robinson

Sue Robinson

Clinical Director/Consultant Psychotherapist
Cassel Hospital

Lead Nurse
Cassel Hospital

Sue Robinson  

Dear Dr Milton

We would be grateful for a small amount of space to respond to Gary Winship's excellent article on psychoanalysis and nursing. But there is a significant omission in his impressively clear digestion of the literature in Britain and in North America.

We would want to emphasise that the proper relationship is the application of psychoanalysis to nursing. Readers of Gary Winship's paper could be left with the view that he is advocating the practice of psychoanalysis by nurses with their in-patient charges. This impression could be gained from his emphasis on, and quotations from, the work of Peplau.

The practice of psychoanalysis (and of psychoanalytic psychotherapy) is through the medium of words in a very circumscribed setting. The practice of nursing is one of activity in a daily living setting. This distinction needs to be sustained. Nurse-counselling on a ward could be informed by psychoanalysis, but it is the practice of neither psychoanalysis nor of nursing. In our view, the challenge that is implicit in Gary Winship's title (Uneasy Alliances?) is to apply the ideas gained from a verbal practice (psychoanalysis) to one based on activity (nursing). This is both a conceptual challenge and a practical one.

In a psychoanalytic setting, more or less all activity contravenes the golden rule, and is therefore deemed, rightly, to be acting-out. In an everyday setting, on the other hand, activity is deeply embedded in the relationships and in the very life of the setting itself. The challenge to psychoanalysis is to conceptualise ordinary activity in an everyday setting as distinct from acting-out. The challenge to nursing is how to make activity with patients as telling a therapeutic modality as verbal interpretation. It needs to be a psycho-social practice.

We were very pleased with Gary Winship's generous comments about the Cassel Hospital's ‘contributions to psychoanalytically-informed nursing’ and our own role in developing this. It seems important to register that Cassel nursing has found a very different way to think about applying psychoanalysis to nursing than to train psychoanalytic nurse-psychotherapists.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Jane Milton

Jane Milton

Jane Milton  

This issue of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy contains a spectrum of papers, from accounts of direct clinical work, to examples of the application of psychoanalytic thinking in a range of Health Service settings. This latter sort of work, where we use our psychoanalytic skills to teach and support other professionals in their work, providing a framework which can give a space for creative thinking, is often neglected in accounts of services. Reviews of the ‘effectiveness of psychotherapy’ should always (but rarely do) take into account this relatively hidden but important and influential part of our work.

Elder's paper, originally given at a meeting in honour of Enid Balint's work, traces the development of the Balints’ work with GPs, and describes, from his own intimate perspective on the work, her influential and distinctive contributions. Lotz gives a useful account of the ways the chaotic inner world of the schizophrenic may be projected and enacted in the acute psychiatric-ward milieu. He then discusses different ways in which a psychoanalytic perspective may be brought in to help. Goodwin's topic is the strain of the dual role of clinician-manager in the NHS, which she examines using psychoanalytic ideas about social defence systems.

Three clinical papers follow. Firstly, Bateman's account of the treatment of an adult patient in whom, Bateman argues, an important but silent type of breakdown, in the form of inappropriate conformism, had taken place in adolescence. Then Kennedy draws on both his work with severely broken-down families at the Cassel Hospital, and an adult analytic case, to discuss clinical issues involved in working with ‘the abused mind’. Finally, Ezquerro gives a lively account of an outpatient psychotherapy group, and his struggles as a trainee with accommodating and making use of two differing theoretical models of group work.

99

[[RunningHead]]

Enid Balint's Contribution to General Practice11

Andrew Elder

Andrew Elder

London, NW8 8EH.

London, NW3 5BA.

Andrew Elder  

The author, a general practitioner himself, gives an appreciation of the work of Enid Balint, with whom he worked closely in her groups for GPs until her death in 1994. He first traces the development of the Balints’, and in particular Enid's, research into, and thinking about, general practice. He touches on the context of this work, in the evolution of applied psychoanalysis, which was a major interest of Enid Balint's. The importance of her conviction that the doctor needed to be helped to develop his own capacities as a doctor, rather than become a therapist, is underlined, and there is some discussion of the ways in which a general practitioner may develop as a result of a Balint group. Finally, the author gives an example from his own practice of a consultation in which he feels the influence of Balint work speaks for itself.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Clinician-Manager Model in the National Health Service: Conflicting Social Defence Systems?

Anne Goodwin

Anne Goodwin

St Nicholas Hospital, Jubilee Road, Gosforth
Newcastle upon Tyne, NE3 3XT.

Anne Goodwin  

Clinicians are increasingly being required to take on additional management duties in today's National Health Service. The framework of social defences provides one way of understanding the role-conflict for these clinician-managers. It is proposed that clinicians cope with the anxieties of health-care work by projecting unacceptable aspects of themselves into managers and that managers develop defensive social structures involving projection into clinicians. The current NHS culture and the associated increased anxiety exacerbates the reliance on these defensive structures. This makes collaboration between clinicians and managers particularly difficult, and results in increased anxiety for those who occupy the dual role.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Jane Milton

Jane Milton

Jane Milton  

For the first time, accompanying this issue of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy is a Supplement consisting of conference proceedings. The APP hopes that this will help the thinking generated at ‘Future Directions of Psychotherapy in the NHS’, held jointly with The Royal College of Psychiatrists in February, to continue and to develop.

The project described by Trijsburg from the Netherlands, based on a questionnaire survey of attitudes to training requirements, particularly concerning the necessity of a substantial training therapy, is of considerable relevance to some of our current preoccupations in the APP. These latter concern the wish to increase the availability of psychoanalytic psychotherapy and the accessibility of trainings, and engage more committed people in the work of the APP, expanding into a regional structure, while at the same time continuing to stand for high standards of training and practice in the profession.

Garelick & Wiener also present an organisation exposed to challenge, and show how struggles within a psychotherapy service towards openness and self-scrutiny in the face of an external review led to some helpful developments.

Chiesa gives us a clinical paper which revisits the old idea of ‘transference as resistance’ from a new angle, showing, with clinical material, how a paranoid, subtly erotised, transference can be used defensively. Shields, who also uses clinical material well to illustrate his theme, is concerned with the meanings of the fee in treatment.

Finally, Temple, Walker & Evans's paper shows us an important example of psychoanalytic psychotherapists willing to struggle at the limits of treatability, with a group of people very stuck with their psychosomatic disorders, in a general hospital setting.

187

responseSet.title: Letter to the Editor
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter to the Editor

Philip Hewitt

Philip Hewitt

Philip Hewitt  

Dear Dr Milton

I read and was moved by David Bell's paper, ‘Primitive Mind of State’ (Volume 10 p. 93). The paper resonates beyond the demise of the National Health Service and now threats to psychotherapy in Health Trusts.

Firstly, I refer to Education, which is even more politically vulnerable than Health, because there has always been an easily exploited split between a service for the poor and one for the rich, something which Health now shares again with Education. Secondly, there are Social Services, which have been decimated in the last fifteen years. Thirdly, the consequences of the Primitive Mind of State resonates through the whole of the Justice system, from the Lord Chief Justice to probation officers, police officers and prison officers.

One thing in particular struck me as it was emphasised by David Bell. This is the issue of ‘survival’ of services: Departments, beliefs, and psychic survival. This made me think about work relating to soldiers who have survived a high probability of death, sight of horrific injuries and loss of comrades. In a theatre of war, there is often a sudden degradation of the individual to ‘survival-only’ mechanisms. By contrast, David Bell gives an account of the chronicity of degradation, culminating with a personal anecdote in which he describes an experience of

… enslavement similar to the dependent relationships inherent in charity.

The survivalism described in the paper is like a perverse relationship to a parent who sadistically plays with meeting or not-meeting the infant's need. David Bell describes a primitive mentality which imposes a relationship to the object without any genuine attempts at understanding. The notion of enslavement reflects our feelings of helplessness, the more so if we are able to remember a reasonable parent of the past who made better provision. The consequence of the primitive mentality described in the paper is a process - which disempowers the individual by transforming justifiable need into unreasonable demand. Then guilt is mobilised and the next step of connoting the need as unreasonable and anti-social is that much easier: for example, ‘single-parent mothers are all scroungers.’ Then, by reducing everything to fiscal policy, the further degradation is that of the bid itself, which was for psychic health and truth. These are some of the complexities with which the primitive mind of our State cannot cope.

responseSet.title: Michael Fordham: Innovations in Analytical Psychology. By James Astor London: Routledge. pp.2.63 £13-99.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Michael Fordham: Innovations in Analytical Psychology. By James Astor London: Routledge. pp.2.63 £13-99.

Roger Hobdell

Roger Hobdell

Roger Hobdell  

This book takes its place in the series ‘Makers of Modern Psychotherapy’. What are Fordham's credentials for such a title?

C. G. Jung created in analytical psychology both a treatment method and a hypothetical developmental theory based on the self and archetypes, most important of which is the pre-oedipal, dual-aspect mother archetype (roughly comparable to Klein's good/bad breast). But, apart from one or two exceptions, Jung did not write about children as children; only the child-within-the-adult. Fordham's task was to study children and find out what stood up to observation and what did not. This was at a time when Jung did not consider child analysis effective. As he famously said to Fordham, ‘If a child was brought to him, he got the mother by the ears (p. 50)’. For Jung, individuation (ie the process whereby the individual differentiates himself from the collective psychology) was an activity of the second half of life. Fordham early on recognised that it is a life-long action, beginning in utero; and he pursued Jung's other view of individuation as ‘development of consciousness out of the original state of identity.’ He was intensely loyal to Jung since his first meeting in the 1930s.

Fordham's own contribution began in the 1940s, when he postulated a primary self, an integrate, out of which were differentiated psyche and soma, conscious and unconscious. The self was seen to play an active part in the formations of mental structures. From the late 1970s, Fordham participated in mother-infant observation seminars that he had set up at the Society of Analytical Psychology and found that what had started as a ‘speculative hypothesis acquired characteristics of fact (p. 56).

James Astor's admirable book has needed to be written for over twenty years, for there has been no introduction to Fordham's work. Had it been written so long ago, however, the evidence for Fordham's hypothesis would have been patchy. Nor would it have included his work on defences of the self (1974), self-objects (1985), and on identity (1995), to name only three of his numerous areas of research. Added to which, Fordham's own memoir is now available and Astor elegantly weaves Fordham's life and meetings with Jung with the generation of his ideas. Astor has eschewed writing a chronological account and instead has produced a thoughtful distillation of his ideas. The main areas are: the self in infancy; ego development; archetypes; transference and countertransference; autism. The last is of particular interest to Fordham since it can reveal how development out of the primary self has gone awry.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Environmentalism: First Thoughts11

Joseph Mishan

Joseph Mishan

St Ann's Hospital
St Ann's Road London N15 3TH.

Joseph Mishan  

This paper attempts to make a case for an affinity between psychoanalysis and the environmental movement. This is evidenced in particular by the emphasis in both on relatedness. Money-Kyrle's paper, ‘The Aim of Psychoanalysis’ is used to emphasise the problems with the recognition of relatedness to our primary objects, and to the natural world. The consequences of such difficulties with recognition are manifest in the external world in continued environmental destruction, and in the internal world in the form of persecutory guilt. It is argued that psychoanalysis can help promote the necessary move into recognition of our relationship to the natural world, which is a narcissistic blow and involves mourning.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mental Space. By Robert M YoungProcess Press. (26 Freegrove Road, N7 9RQ) Pp. 200 #15.95 + #1.50 postage

David Morgan

David Morgan

David Morgan  

It is a hard task to do justice in a review to a book with such a wide remit as the definition and understanding of mental space. In these present times, when the need

89

for reflection, thought, and an awareness of the complexity of life are constantly threatened by the requirement for models of mind that allow for measurement, quantification, and above all cost-effectiveness, it is unfashionable to admit that human problems do not change as a result of quick solutions. The creation of a mental space where these difficulties can be deeply understood is not an easy matter.

It is Robert Young's thesis that mental space occurs at the interface of psychology, culture, politics and philosophy. He states

Analytical work with its emphasis on the importance of mental space is the last refuge of those who wish to change the world but have learned that to change oneself and perhaps a few others is quite a lot to be getting on with.

This move, from an omnipotent position to a clearer understanding of what is really possible, taking into account one's own nature and that of the Other, lies at the heart of this book. Throughout it, one feels that Young's idea of what he can achieve has, with experience and training, become more realistic than it was before. This development seems to have been a painful process, and one can still at times feel his wish to burst out of these confines and write the book that will solve all problems. But this impulse is checked, and the rather verbose theorising that I feel is the book's weakness is far outweighed by its perceptiveness. What seem at first like rather overblown statements give real gems of insight. This is particularly so in relation to the field of applied psychoanalysis: light is thrown on difficult areas such as politics, racism, and the dynamics of groups and institutions.

Reading Young's book, with its emphasis on the space to think, I reflected that we are at present facing a period in social and political life in which the importance of mental space is under attack and in which the emphasis is on thoughtless expediency. People can grow to feel that they live in a world saturated by actions, lacking any cultural or mental space to find any form of containing experience. Young acknowledges the pessimism in both Freud and Klein about humankind's capacity to manage these destructive impulses; and he explores those difficulties in personal, social and cultural contexts, often drawing on his own experiences. His background as a philosopher and scientist who came later to psychoanalysis helped him to put psychoanalysis in it cultural and philosophical context. He sees psychoanalysis as lying in the middle of the usual mind-body dualism that has dominated so much of our thinking in both philosophy and the sciences. In this it is a kind of Flying Dutchman, for ever sailing the seas, between Body and Mind and between Science and the Humanities, never finding a safe haven in any of them. The need for certainties can be paramount in some of these disciplines; and the great strength — but therefore weakness, from a scientific point of view — of psychoanalysis is that it dares to bear and explore uncertainties that do not lend themselves to absolute truths. It defies linguistic and scientific normalisation.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Hostage of the Fee: Meanings of Money, Countertransference, and the Beginning Therapist

John Shields PHD

John Shields

235 Montgomery Street, Suite 841, San Francisco, CA 94104.

John Shields  

The issue of money and fees for treatment is one of the most inadequately discussed issues in the psychotherapy literature. Despite its relevance to every psychotherapy, and its irrefutable impact on the psychotherapeutic relationship, there appears to be a resistance among us to exploring this issue either in our literature or in our training. This discussion highlights some of the meanings of fees in the arena of psychoanalytic psychotherapy, and poses some possible explanations for its absence from our literature and training. Case material is presented from the author's beginning psychotherapy practice. Transference and countertransference issues are discussed, as are some more general experiences and impressions of the author having to do with money, values, and the ethics of professional practice regarding fees. The importance of more open discussion, especially among beginning therapists, and therapists in training, about money and fees, as well as their countertransference implications and transference influences on the therapy, is emphasised. It is argued that with the increasing number of cases involving third-party interference, it is imperative that the issue of fees, and its impact on the therapeutic process receive more attention in training.

responseSet.abstract:

Primitive Mind of State11

David Bell

David Bell

Flat 4, Mnllion Court
Finchley Road, London NW3 5JH.

David Bell  

This paper examines the introduction of the Market into the National Health Service, viewing it within the perspective of the destruction of the Welfare consensus. It is suggested that the ideology of the Market and the attack on welfare-ism derives considerable support from their appeal to primitive parts of the personality that view dependency or vulnerability as weakness, the process originally described by Rosenfeld who termed it ‘destructive narcissism’. The paper develops this theme and uses a clinical example to illustrate the phenomenon. It is also suggested the NHS reforms create fragmentation and alienation. This has led to primitive survivalism which, although a natural outcome of the process described, will prove very costly in terms of its effects on morale — an essential component of adequate health-care delivery.

The Supervisory Encounter. By D. JacobsP. DavidD.J. Mayer. Holland New Haven & London: Yale University Press. Pp.182Doing Supervision and Being Supervised. By R. Langs. London: Karnac. pp. 269

Victor Sedlak

Victor Sedlak

Victor Sedlak  

The publication of these two books on supervision reflects a growing interest in what has been a relatively neglected area of psychotherapeutic experience. As Jacobs and his colleagues point out, supervision is often felt by the supervisor to be a break from clinical responsibilities rather than an important part of a senior practitioner's work needing careful thought and expertise. In his contribution Langs also stresses that the paucity of thought that has gone into the issue of supervision has tremendously important and often detrimental consequences on the subsequent functioning of the supervisee and his or her treatment of patients. Despite this initial agreement, it is difficult to imagine two books written on the same topic that are so different in their content and style.

Those who know the considerable body of Robert Langs's work in the last twenty years will recognise many familiar concerns and themes. Uppermost amongst these is his emphasis on the importance of the frame of a setting, be it for supervision or for therapy. He gives an excellent description of this, including, amongst other variables, a fixed time, fee, and meeting-place which must be quiet, private and used solely for professional practice. He attaches great importance to the issue of a supervisor's anonymity and the confidentiality of the supervisory relationship. Much of his subsequent argument is that grossly insufficient attention is paid in most clinical and supervisory settings to the effect that modifications of the frame or parameters have on the supervisory and clinical encounters. For this contribution alone, I think this book is worthwhile.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Paranoid Transference as Resistance to Progress

Marco Chiesa

Marco Chiesa

The Cassel Hospital, Department of Research & Education
Richmond, Surrey TW10 7JF.

Marco Chiesa  

In this paper I argue that the original Freudian concept of transference as an obstacle against the work of analysis is still valid if applied to specific types of transference manifestations. After outlining the theoretical development of the concept of transference from Freud's time to the present, and briefly discussing the notion of resistance, I will present clinical material from patients who develop paranoid transferences during the course of treatment. I hope to demonstrate how one of the functions of this type of transference is to stifle analytic work and to engage the analyst in an unproductive and sterile interaction.

responseSet.authorCitation: Temple, N., Walker, J. & Evans, M.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Group Psychotherapy with Psychosomatic and Somatising Patients in a General Hospital

Nicholas Temple , Jill Walker and Marcus Evans

Marcus Evans

120 Belsize Lane, London NW3 5BA.

Denmark Hill, London SE5 8AZ.

Maudsley Hospital.

Marcus Evans  

This paper describes a pilot study of the treatment of psychosomatic and somatising patients who were referred to the Psychiatric Outpatients of a general hospital because of difficulties in their management. The patients were offered treatment in a once-weekly psychotherapy group. The treatment approach was aimed at improving the patients’ capacity to experience emotional problems, and challenged the tendency to displace all their difficulties into physical symptoms and illness. At times, the group was effective in achieving these aims despite strong resistance from the patients. The experience of the conflictful feelings was accompanied by high levels of anxiety and fears of destructiveness which demonstrated both the protective function of the somatic symptoms and the need for more intensive group therapy to provide sufficient support for them to be worked through.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Understanding Mental Objects. By Meir Perlow. Foreword by Joseph SandlerNew Library of Psychoanalysis, No. 22. London: Routledge. pp. 180 £40.00 hb; £14.99 pb.

Susan Budd

Susan Budd

Susan Budd  

The best introduction to this densely-argued analysis of the concept of mental objects is provided by the brief Conclusion. In it, the author summarises how he has traced the concept of a mental object from being an aspect of the drives, as it was in early Freud, and shows how it was added to and made more complex by the growing influence of both the developmental approach characteristic of ego-psychology, and the more active concept of mental object as phantasy. The book sticks as closely as it can to elucidating this single, very important, concept, whilst alluding briefly to the implications that ways of seeing it have for the whole body of psychoanalytic theory of which it is one of the basic building-blocks. Given the inherent complexity and abstraction of its subject-matter, the style is exemplary - clear, concise, and with a wealth of bibliographical references.

Meir Perlow says, with justice, that the

… aim of this delineation, and of the historical survey and the discussion of theoretical issues that led up to it, is to further our understanding of the plethora of ways in which the mind structures its interactions with people.

(p. 165).

He also states that he has merely tried to map the different ideas of various theorists, and the ties and differences between them, rather than prefer one to another — but this seems not quite so true. His sympathies seem to lie more with the ego-psychological tradition, perhaps because this is where more careful and scrupulous attention has been given to the meanings of terms and their theoretical implications, than has been done with the experience-near and descriptive use of terms, which is more characteristic of the object-relations tradition, and of writers such as Kohut and Winnicott who put the self and orientations to the self at the centre of their work. I think that he is justified, though, in pointing out that the vivid and convincing clinical descriptions of the latter two groups are often not theories at all, but can serve to conceal fundamental theoretical difficulties and ambiguities. He shows us how these theorists bounced ideas off one another, so to speak. For example, he describes the impetus that Melanie Klein's writings gave to American ego-psychologists such as Schafer to return to metapsychology, which had fallen out of favour, and to try to account for her clinical data in terms of action language, which used concepts drawn from ego-psychology.
